strict_win_deltas.delta: P2 confines the whole feature to the Job class, mirroring the existing cancel_after/_is_overdue pattern with a parallel _is_run_limit_reached helper, whereas P3 also rewrites Scheduler._run_job to wrap job.run() in a try/except BaseException block, spreading the concern across two classes.
P2 delivers a complete max_runs() implementation with validation, cancellation logic, and tests in schedule/__init__.py and test_schedule.py, while P1's diff is completely empty.
P3 delivers a working max_runs() implementation spanning Job and Scheduler._run_job with passing tests, while P1's diff contains no code changes whatsoever.
P2 keeps max-run cancellation localized in Job.run via _is_run_limit_reached(), while P3 adds a Scheduler._run_job try/except that reaches into job._is_max_runs_reached().
P2 adds Job.max_runs(), per-job state, cancellation checks, tests, and docs, while P1's diff is empty.
P3 adds a chainable Job.max_runs() implementation with validation, counters, docs, and tests, while P1 contains no implementation.

P3 increments self._runs before calling self.job_func() and adds a try/except in Scheduler._run_job so a job that raises on its nth call is still canceled (test_max_runs_counts_failing_job_execution), while P2 increments self._run_count only after job_func() returns, so a raising call never counts toward the budget.

P3 ships tests like test_max_runs_cancels_after_run_pending_budget and test_max_runs_counts_failing_job_execution proving the cancellation and error paths behave correctly, while P1 has no implementation or tests at all.

P2 ships test_max_runs_cancels_after_budget_with_run_pending and test_max_runs_and_until_cancel_on_first_limit demonstrating correct run_pending/until interplay, while P1 provides nothing to evaluate.
P3 increments _runs before job_func() and cancels in Scheduler._run_job's exception path, while P2 increments _run_count only after job_func() returns.
P3 covers run_pending(), run_all(), validation, chaining before the time unit, per-job budgets, until interaction, and failing executions in tests, while P1 has no tests or behavior.
P2 covers run_pending(), run_all(), validation, last-call-wins, per-job budgets, until interaction, and next_run/idle_seconds removal behavior, while P1 has no coverage.

P2 updates all three doc surfaces touched by comparable Job methods — README.rst, docs/examples.rst, and docs/index.rst — while P3 leaves docs/index.rst's mirrored Usage block un-updated even though it duplicates the same example list changed in README.rst.

P2 implements and tests every enumerated requirement (chainable setter in both positions, run_pending/run_all coverage, until first-limit-wins, next_run/idle_seconds reflection, validation, per-job isolation, docs) while P1's empty diff satisfies none of them.
P3 implements and tests nearly every enumerated requirement including both chain orderings (test_max_runs_can_be_chained_before_time_unit) and until interaction, while P1 implements nothing.
P2 documents the new API in README.rst, docs/examples.rst, and docs/index.rst, while P3 omits docs/index.rst.
P2 implements and tests the requested successful execution budget semantics across run_pending(), run_all(), until(), validation, and per-job isolation, while P1 leaves the feature absent.
P3 implements and tests the requested scheduler-driven budget cancellation behavior, while P1 leaves every requested behavior absent.

P2's max_runs() stays confined to Job with a docstring matching the concise style of neighboring methods like .until(), while P3 also requires future readers to reason about a newly added except BaseException: ... raise clause in Scheduler._run_job, an unusually broad except clause for this codebase.
P2 adds a documented, tested max_runs() API consistent with the existing Job fluent-chain methods (README.rst, docs/examples.rst, docs/index.rst all updated), while P1 adds no API and no docs at all.
P3 adds a documented, tested max_runs() API with a docstring explaining the exception-bubbling contract, while P1's diff is empty and adds nothing for maintainers to use.
P2 adds the public API without coupling Scheduler._run_job to a private Job max-run helper, while P3 introduces that cross-class private-state dependency.
P2 provides the chainable max_runs(n) setter with ValueError validation and fluent usage examples, while P1 provides no API surface.
P3 provides the chainable max_runs(n) setter, validation, and usage documentation, while P1 has no ergonomic surface at all.

P1 keeps `comments=comments` as its own named argument beside `description=description` and `categories=...`, matching the call's existing one-parameter-per-line style, while P2 drops `comments` from the explicit argument list and buries it inside the `**item_extra_kwargs` spread.

P1's `item_extra_kwargs.pop('comments')` branch explicitly reconciles the new `item_comments` hook with the pre-existing `item_extra_kwargs()` 'detour' the ticket names, whereas P3 just appends `comments=self._get_dynamic_attr('item_comments', item),` next to `**self.item_extra_kwargs(item)` with no acknowledgment the two can supply the same key.

P2's `if 'comments' not in item_extra_kwargs` guard still integrates the new hook with the `item_extra_kwargs()` extension point, while P3 adds the `comments` kwarg as a bare, unconditional sibling of `**self.item_extra_kwargs(item)` with no such check.

P1 adds an explicit `comments=comments` argument to `feed.add_item()` after resolving `item_comments`, while P2 hides the new hook by injecting `'comments'` into `item_extra_kwargs` before `**item_extra_kwargs`.

P1 reconciles the new direct `item_comments` hook with the existing `'comments'` detour by popping that key from `item_extra_kwargs`, while P3 adds `comments=...` directly before `**self.item_extra_kwargs(item)`.

P2 preserves existing `item_extra_kwargs` comments values by checking `'comments' not in item_extra_kwargs`, while P3 always passes `comments=self._get_dynamic_attr(...)` alongside `**self.item_extra_kwargs(item)`.

P1 calls `dict(self.item_extra_kwargs(item))` before popping `comments` out of it, guaranteeing the object returned by a subclass's `item_extra_kwargs()` is never mutated even if that method returns a cached/shared dict, while P2 reassigns the bare return value and only avoids mutating it because no `.pop()`/`.update()` happens to be called on it.

P1's pop-if-present/else-dynamic-attr logic guarantees `feed.add_item()` is never called with two values for `comments`, whereas P3's bare `comments=self._get_dynamic_attr('item_comments', item),` next to `**self.item_extra_kwargs(item)` raises `TypeError: got multiple values for keyword argument 'comments'` whenever an existing subclass's `item_extra_kwargs()` already returns `'comments'`.

P2's `if 'comments' not in item_extra_kwargs` check prevents the same duplicate-keyword collision when `**item_extra_kwargs` is spread into `feed.add_item()`, while P3 has no such guard and crashes under the identical scenario.
P1 adds a dedicated regression test for `TestRss2FeedWithCommentsFromExtraKwargs`, while P2 only tests the new `item_comments` hook path.

P1 avoids duplicate keyword errors by removing `'comments'` from the copied extra kwargs before passing `comments=comments`, while P3 would collide when `item_extra_kwargs()` returns a `comments` key.
P2 checks for an existing `'comments'` key before adding the dynamic hook value, while P3 unconditionally combines `comments=...` with arbitrary `**self.item_extra_kwargs(item)`.

P1 ships `TestRss2FeedWithCommentsFromExtraKwargs` plus `test_rss2_feed_with_comments_from_extra_kwargs` to lock in that an existing `item_extra_kwargs()`-supplied `comments` value still wins, while P2 implements the identical precedence in `views.py` but adds no test exercising that branch.

P1's `/syndication/rss2/comments-extra/` fixture proves the old `item_extra_kwargs` 'detour' mentioned in the ticket keeps working after `item_comments` is added, while P3's test changes only cover the new `item_comments` attribute and never test the `item_extra_kwargs` interaction at all.

P2's `if 'comments' not in item_extra_kwargs` merge keeps the previously-working `item_extra_kwargs()`-based comments path functioning going forward, while P3 adds no equivalent check anywhere in `views.py` or its tests.
P1 documents that `item_comments` is only applicable to RSS feeds, while P2's new documentation describes the comments URL hook without that RSS-only constraint.
P1 keeps the existing `item_extra_kwargs` comments detour working and tests it, while P3's direct keyword addition breaks that explicitly mentioned migration path.
P2 uses a realistic per-item comments URL in its test feed, while P3 sets `item_comments` to the unrelated copyright string `'Copyright (c) 2007, Sally Smith'`.

P1's `feed.add_item()` call still lists `comments=comments,` explicitly next to every other named argument, so a maintainer sees it at the call site, whereas P2's call only spreads `**item_extra_kwargs`, making `comments` invisible there and discoverable only by tracing the preceding `if` block.
P1's explicit if/else over `item_extra_kwargs`/`item_comments` documents in code which source wins on conflict, while P3 gives no such signal and the conflict only surfaces as a runtime `TypeError`.

P2's `if 'comments' not in item_extra_kwargs` at least documents in code that the two sources can collide, while P3's single added `comments=self._get_dynamic_attr('item_comments', item),` line gives a future maintainer no indication that pairing it with an `item_extra_kwargs()` returning `'comments'` will crash.
P1 makes `comments` a visible first-class `feed.add_item()` argument in `views.py`, while P2 makes future readers discover it indirectly inside the constructed `item_extra_kwargs` mapping.
P1 separates compatibility handling from the new API by copying and popping `item_extra_kwargs`, while P3 leaves the old and new APIs competing for the same `comments` keyword.
P2 centralizes precedence by only adding the hook value when extras lack `comments`, while P3 provides no precedence rule for users who already supply `comments` through `item_extra_kwargs()`.

P2 adds explicit dependency tuples (app_label, model_name, '_order', True) resolved via a new AlterOrderWithRespectTo branch in the dependency-satisfaction check, staying inside the autodetector's existing dependency-graph idiom, whereas P1 achieves ordering purely by relocating generate_altered_order_with_respect_to() earlier in the top-level call sequence in _detect_changes and relying on insertion-order tie-breaking in the topological sort.

P2 factors index/constraint field-and-expression detection into one reusable _get_dependencies_for_generated_fields() helper wired into generate_added_indexes, generate_added_constraints, and both CreateModel loops, whereas P3 builds a bespoke _index_references_order() helper plus a new 'order_wrt_set' dependency-type string that is wired only into the index code paths.

P1's single reorder of two code blocks uniformly protects both the CreateModel indexes loop and the constraints loop with one change, whereas P3 introduces a new dependency-type sentinel and helper but never touches generate_added_constraints or the CreateModel constraints loop, leaving that path structurally unaddressed.

P2 models the ordering as explicit dependencies via _get_dependencies_for_generated_fields(), while P1 relies on moving generate_altered_order_with_respect_to() earlier in the whole generation sequence.
P2 reuses the existing dependency mechanism with a generated _order dependency, while P3 introduces a bespoke order_wrt_set dependency kind only for this case.
P1 consistently reorders AlterOrderWithRespectTo before indexes, constraints, unique_together, and index_together, while P3 only adds special dependencies for AddIndex operations.

P2's helper also inspects index.condition and constraint.check/condition via model._get_expr_references(), so an Index(condition=Q(_order__gte=0)) or CheckConstraint(check=Q(_order__gte=0)) is correctly ordered after '_order' is created, a case P1 never detects and only happens to satisfy because its reorder is unconditional rather than reasoned about field references.

P2 adds '_order' dependency tracking to generate_added_constraints and the CreateModel constraints loop via the same helper, while P3's '_index_references_order' check and 'order_wrt_set' dependency are consulted only inside generate_created_models's index loop and generate_added_indexes, leaving AddConstraint operations referencing '_order' unprotected against the same crash.

P1's reorder in generate_created_models unconditionally emits AlterOrderWithRespectTo before both the index loop and the 'for constraint in constraints' loop, so any constraint referencing '_order' is also safely ordered, whereas P3 special-cases only AddIndex operations and leaves AddConstraint operations for the same model with no '_order'-aware dependency at all.

P2 detects _order references in index fields, include fields, index conditions, constraint fields, constraint includes, constraint conditions, and check expressions, while P1 only changes operation ordering.
P2 covers constraints and expression references through model._get_expr_references(), while P3's _index_references_order() only checks index.fields and index.include.
P1's tests cover constraints plus unique_together and index_together involving _order, while P3 has no handling or tests for constraints or together options.

P2's tests test_set_alter_order_with_respect_to_before_condition_index_constraint and test_add_model_order_with_respect_to_condition_index_constraint pin down behavior for Q(_order__gte=0)-style expression references, a regression net P1's test_add_model_order_with_respect_to_index_constraint and test_set_order_with_respect_to_index_constraint_together never assert.

P2's added tests assert exact operation ordering for both AddIndex and AddConstraint across create and alter paths, whereas P3's test_set_alter_order_with_respect_to_index and test_add_model_order_with_respect_to_index cover indexes only and add no constraint-referencing-'_order' regression test at all.

P1's test_set_order_with_respect_to_index_constraint_together exercises order_with_respect_to combined with unique_together, index_together, indexes, and constraints simultaneously and asserts the full resulting operation order, a broader combined-option surface than P3's index-only tests which never touch constraints, unique_together, or index_together.
P2 makes future database operations depending on generated fields sortable by dependency, while P1 bakes in a fixed generator order that can be disturbed by later operation categories.
P2 handles both newly created models and later-added indexes or constraints that reference _order, while P3 handles only indexes and misses constraint/check-condition variants.
P1 accounts for AddConstraint in the created-model path and adds tests for it, while P3 never changes AddConstraint dependency handling.

P2 keeps the fix entirely inside dependency-declaration code (one new helper plus one new elif branch) without touching the master method-call sequence in _detect_changes, whereas P1 permanently relocates self.generate_altered_order_with_respect_to() in that sequence, a global reordering whose rationale isn't localized to the '_order'-referencing-index case it addresses.

P2 exposes one general-purpose helper (_get_dependencies_for_generated_fields) callable from all four index/constraint generation sites, whereas P3 spreads the fix across a new 'order_wrt_set' string literal, a new _index_references_order static method, and duplicated old/new-model-state lookups inside generate_added_indexes.

P1's change is confined to relocating two already-existing code blocks with no new dependency-type vocabulary, whereas P3 adds a new magic string 'order_wrt_set' as a dependency-tuple sentinel alongside the pre-existing True/'order_wrt_unset' conventions, growing the implicit protocol the dependency-resolution code must handle.

P2 centralizes generated-field dependency discovery in _get_dependencies_for_generated_fields(), while P1 duplicates ordering concerns by moving calls in both generate_changes() and generate_created_models().
P2's helper accepts field names and expressions for reuse by indexes and constraints, while P3's _index_references_order(index) is narrowly tied to Index internals.
P1 avoids adding a new dependency sentinel string, while P3 adds the custom 'order_wrt_set' dependency mode to _satisfies_dependency().
